fast.summary: This survey comprehensively reviews LLM alignment techniques—from supervised fine-tuning to preference-based methods like DPO and Constitutional AI—covering training paradigms, evaluation frameworks, and open challenges. It synthesizes the current state of practice across leading AI labs and identifies key trade-offs between alignment objectives.
fast.key_idea: Preference-based alignment methods (e.g., DPO, RLHF) outperform basic supervised fine-tuning for capturing nuanced human intent, but introduce new challenges around reward misspecification, distributional robustness, and scalable oversight.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners building or deploying LLMs need a structured understanding of which alignment techniques to apply, their failure modes, and how to evaluate them—this survey provides a consolidated reference for making those engineering and policy decisions.
possible_use_cases: Selecting the right alignment training paradigm (SFT vs. DPO vs. RLHF) for a production LLM deployment
Benchmarking and auditing LLM safety using the reviewed evaluation frameworks and datasets
Identifying open research problems in value pluralism and continuous alignment for academic or R&D roadmap planning

deep.method_breakdown: This is a survey paper that systematically reviews alignment techniques across multiple paradigms: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for basic instruction-following, preference-based methods (RLHF, DPO) for nuanced human intent alignment, and constitutional/brain-inspired approaches. It characterizes trade-offs between alignment objectives and evaluates existing benchmarking frameworks for measuring alignment quality, robustness, and scalable oversight.
deep.key_innovation: The survey's distinguishing contribution is its introduction of Alignment Uncertainty Quantification (AUQ) as a lens for analyzing alignment methods — treating alignment not as a binary property but as a probabilistic one with measurable uncertainty. It also synthesizes brain-inspired alignment methods alongside classical ML paradigms, a relatively underexplored comparison in prior surveys.
deep.technical_insight: The most critical design insight surfaced is that preference-based methods (especially DPO) outperform SFT for nuanced alignment because they optimize directly on relative human judgments rather than absolute labels, sidestepping the reward misspecification problem inherent in RLHF's reward model intermediary. DPO collapses the reward model into the policy update, reducing compounding error.
deep.limitations: As a survey, it does not produce new empirical results. Key limitations it identifies in the field include: reward misspecification (reward models proxy but don't capture true human values), distributional robustness failures under distribution shift, scalable oversight bottlenecks (human feedback doesn't scale to superhuman capability regimes), and value pluralism — alignment methods implicitly assume a single human value function, ignoring cross-cultural and individual variation.
interview_questions: What is reward misspecification and why is it a fundamental problem for RLHF-based alignment?
How does Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) differ mechanically from PPO-based RLHF, and what trade-offs does it introduce?
What is Constitutional AI and how does it reduce dependence on human-labeled preference data?
How would you design an alignment evaluation benchmark that is robust to Goodhart's Law?
What does 'scalable oversight' mean and why does it become critical as models approach or exceed human-level capability?
interview_answers: Reward misspecification occurs when the learned reward model captures a proxy of human intent rather than true intent — the model then optimizes the proxy to an extreme, producing reward hacking. For example, a summarization model rewarded for fluency may produce confident-sounding hallucinations. It is fundamental because any reward model trained on finite human labels will have gaps that a powerful optimizer will exploit.

PPO-RLHF trains a separate reward model on preference pairs, then uses PPO to optimize policy against it with a KL-divergence penalty. DPO re-derives the optimal policy analytically and shows the reward model can be expressed implicitly via the policy itself, yielding a single classification-style loss on preference pairs. DPO is simpler and more stable but can underfit subtle preferences and lacks the online exploration that PPO's rollouts provide.

Constitutional AI (Anthropic) uses a set of written principles (a 'constitution') to have the model self-critique and revise its outputs in a red-team/revision loop, generating synthetic preference data without human labelers for every example. A smaller set of human labels defines the constitution; the model then scales preference learning via self-supervision. This reduces annotation cost but inherits biases baked into the constitution's authors.

A robust benchmark should: (1) use held-out tasks not represented in training distributions, (2) include adversarial prompts probing known failure modes, (3) measure calibration and refusal quality not just helpfulness, (4) periodically rotate evaluation sets to prevent contamination, and (5) include diverse rater pools across cultures to surface value pluralism failures rather than assuming a single ground truth.

Scalable oversight refers to the challenge of supervising AI systems whose outputs humans cannot fully evaluate — e.g., a model writing 10,000-line codebases or doing novel scientific reasoning. Current RLHF assumes humans can judge output quality, which breaks down at superhuman capability. Proposed solutions include debate (two AI agents argue, human judges the argument), recursive reward modeling (decompose tasks into human-verifiable subtasks), and AI-assisted oversight where a weaker model helps humans evaluate a stronger one.
